questions: An early-2000s beginner-level UNIX/Linux tutorial authored by a UK academic was released under a Creative Commons license that allows users to copy, redistribute, remix, and adapt the material, provided they give appropriate credit, avoid commercial use, and license derivative works under identical terms. Identify the exact Creative Commons license version used for this tutorial and provide a comprehensive summary of: (1) the freedoms granted, (2) the specific conditions imposed on users, (3) any statements about additional restrictions and warranties, (4) the canonical URL of the license, and (5) the notice regarding the recommended newer license version.

An early-2000s beginner UNIX/Linux tutorial states that it is distributed under an older Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike 2.0 license and includes a notice recommending the use of the newer 4.0 versions of Creative Commons licenses instead. Identify (1) the 4.0 International license that preserves the same core elements of attribution, noncommercial use, and share-alike requirements, and (2) the 4.0 International license that requires only attribution and permits commercial use without a share-alike condition. For both licenses, provide their canonical URLs and summarize: the freedoms granted (sharing and adapting), whether commercial use is allowed, whether share-alike is required, the "no additional restrictions" clause, and the standard legal/disclaimer notice regarding the deed and Creative Commons’ role.

You are preparing a UNIX/Linux tutorial for beginners and want to release it under a ShareAlike Creative Commons license so others can adapt and redistribute it. To ensure compliance when others remix your work, analyze the official compatibility rules for the current 4.0 versions of the two ShareAlike licenses (the commercial and the noncommercial variants). 

First, identify which non-CC licenses (if any) have been officially declared compatible with the 4.0 version of the commercial ShareAlike license, and explain any special conditions that apply to those compatibilities. Then clarify whether any non-CC licenses are currently compatible with the 4.0 version of the noncommercial ShareAlike license. 

Next, describe the mission of the nonprofit organization that stewards these licenses and summarize the types of tools it provides to support the global knowledge commons. 

Finally, if you wanted formal training before licensing and distributing your Linux tutorial, outline the main certificate tracks offered by this organization, including their target audiences, course structure (duration and format), and the available bootcamp delivery options.

You are preparing to publish a free “UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners” and want to ensure it can be legally shared, remixed, and reused worldwide. Identify the nonprofit organization that both (a) develops and stewards the globally used open licenses and public domain tools commonly applied to educational and technical materials, and (b) operates a structured certificate training program with sector-specific courses (including for educators, academic librarians, and cultural heritage professionals). Based on publicly available information, explain this organization’s mission, describe its core legal and technical tools (including its standard licenses, public domain dedication/marking tools, and its newer framework for guiding AI reuse), and outline how it supports implementation across sectors through training, institutional adoption, and capacity-building resources. Answer length: 300-400 words.

rounds.question: An early-2000s beginner-level UNIX/Linux tutorial authored by a UK academic was released under a Creative Commons license that allows users to copy, redistribute, remix, and adapt the material, provided they give appropriate credit, avoid commercial use, and license derivative works under identical terms. Identify the exact Creative Commons license version used for this tutorial and provide a comprehensive summary of: (1) the freedoms granted, (2) the specific conditions imposed on users, (3) any statements about additional restrictions and warranties, (4) the canonical URL of the license, and (5) the notice regarding the recommended newer license version.
checklist_width: Freedom to Share (copy and redistribute in any medium or format)
Freedom to Adapt (remix, transform, and build upon the material)
Attribution requirement (appropriate credit, link to license, indicate changes)
NonCommercial restriction (no commercial use)
ShareAlike requirement (derivatives under same license)
No additional restrictions clause
Statement about no warranties and possible other rights (privacy, publicity, moral rights)
Canonical URL: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/
Notice recommending use of the 4.0 version as more user-friendly and internationally robust
checklist_depth: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0) identified as the specific license
Logical proof: Matches conditions of attribution + noncommercial + share-alike, and corresponds to an older pre-4.0 license version with canonical URL ending in /2.0/
rounds.rationale: The question uses contextual clues (early-2000s UNIX/Linux tutorial by a UK academic under a CC license with attribution, noncommercial, and share-alike terms) to force identification of the specific CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 license without naming it directly (Deep logic). It then requires aggregation of multiple scattered elements from the official license deed—freedoms, conditions, disclaimers, URL, and version comparison—ensuring broad information synthesis (Wide scope).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Notice This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust . If
rounds.answer_a:
The tutorial utilizes the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales** license [1]. This license grants users the freedom to **Share** (copy, distribute, and transmit the work) and to **Remix** (adapt the work), provided specific conditions are met [2].

Users must adhere to three conditions: **Attribution** (credit must be given to the original author in the manner specified), **NonCommercial** (the work may not be used for commercial purposes), and **ShareAlike** (derivative works must be licensed under identical terms) [2]. The license includes explicit statements that any of the aforementioned conditions can be waived with permission from the copyright holder. Furthermore, the rights granted are subject to the user's fair dealing or fair use rights, and the work is provided "as-is" without any warranties regarding accuracy or reliability [3].

The canonical URL for this license is `https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/` [2]. Currently, the license deed displays a prominent notice stating that this is a **deprecated license** and recommends that users consider adopting a current license version, such as the 4.0 Unported license, for new works [2].

## References
[1] Mike Joy, "Introduction to Unix," University of Warwick, 2004. https://warwick.ac.uk/fac/sci/dcs/teaching/material/unix/
[2] Creative Commons, "Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales," 2004. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/
[3] Creative Commons, "Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales Legal Code," 2004. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/legalcode
rounds.answer_b:
The search results identify that M. Stonebank's UNIX Tutorial for Beginners (circa 2001) from the University of Surrey was released under a Creative Commons license[1][2][3], but the provided search results do not specify the exact license version, its canonical URL, or detailed terms regarding freedoms granted, specific conditions, warranty disclaimers, or mentions of newer license versions.

The results confirm the tutorial is a beginner-level UNIX/Linux guide from a UK academic released in early 2000s under Creative Commons licensing[3][4], with one academic institution noting it was "available for download" and "copied" for educational reuse purposes[3]. One related resource in the search results—the Linux Shell Scripting Tutorial—specifies use of "Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial-Share Alike 3.0 Unported"[5], suggesting CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 was common for educational materials from this period.

However, to provide the comprehensive information requested—including (1) the exact freedoms granted, (2) specific conditions imposed, (3) warranty statements, (4) the canonical license URL, and (5) notices regarding newer versions—would require access to either the original tutorial's metadata or the actual Creative Commons license deed, which are not contained in these search results.

The search results confirm the tutorial exists and uses Creative Commons licensing but do not provide sufficient detail to answer the specific technical questions about license version, exact terms, canonical URL, and deprecation notices.

## References

[1] https://www.eecs.yorku.ca/course_archive/2019-20/W/2031M/posts/UnixIntroduction2019.pdf
[2] https://info-ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[3] https://linuxclass.heinz.cmu.edu/doc/Unix-Tutorial-surrey/
[4] http://www.cs.unc.edu/~montek/teaching/Comp411-Fall17/UnixTut/unixintro.html
[5] https://staff.cs.manchester.ac.uk/~fumie/internal/LinuxShellScriptingTutorialv2.0.pdf
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the license as Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 (UK variant), which aligns with CC BY-NC-SA 2.0. Agent B fails to identify the exact license version and instead speculates about 3.0, explicitly stating insufficient information—this is a DEEP failure. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A covers most required elements—freedoms (share/remix), conditions (attribution, noncommercial, share-alike), waiver possibility, no-warranty statement, canonical URL (though it uses the UK port URL rather than the general /2.0/ URL in the checklist), and a deprecation notice recommending 4.0. However, it omits explicit mention of the "No additional restrictions" clause and the statement about other rights (privacy, publicity, moral rights), and provides a slightly different canonical URL than required. These are WIDE gaps but not catastrophic. Agent B provides none of the requested structured license details, failing most checklist items.

Finally, User Experience: Agent A is structured, scannable, citation-supported, and directly answers the query. Agent B is effectively a refusal with light speculation, offering minimal utility. 

Because Agent A has minor sub-point omissions and a URL mismatch, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. However, it clearly outperforms Agent B, which failed the core identification. Hence: A_BETTER.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: DEEP.

rounds.question: An early-2000s beginner UNIX/Linux tutorial states that it is distributed under an older Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike 2.0 license and includes a notice recommending the use of the newer 4.0 versions of Creative Commons licenses instead. Identify (1) the 4.0 International license that preserves the same core elements of attribution, noncommercial use, and share-alike requirements, and (2) the 4.0 International license that requires only attribution and permits commercial use without a share-alike condition. For both licenses, provide their canonical URLs and summarize: the freedoms granted (sharing and adapting), whether commercial use is allowed, whether share-alike is required, the "no additional restrictions" clause, and the standard legal/disclaimer notice regarding the deed and Creative Commons’ role.
checklist_width: Canonical URL for the Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International license
Freedoms granted (share and adapt) under the Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 license
Statement that commercial use is not allowed under the Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 license
Statement that ShareAlike distribution is required under the Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 license
Inclusion of the 'No additional restrictions' clause for the Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 license
Canonical URL for the Attribution 4.0 International license
Freedoms granted (share and adapt) under the Attribution 4.0 license, including commercial use
Statement that commercial use is allowed under the Attribution 4.0 license
Statement that no ShareAlike requirement exists under the Attribution 4.0 license
Inclusion of the 'No additional restrictions' clause for the Attribution 4.0 license
Summary of the standard disclaimer that the deed is not a license and has no legal value
Statement that Creative Commons is not a law firm and provides no legal services
checklist_depth: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0) + matches the older BY-NC-SA 2.0 elements (attribution, noncommercial, share-alike)
Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) + identified as the attribution-only, more permissive license allowing commercial use without share-alike
rounds.rationale: The question uses the contextual clue of a UNIX tutorial referencing an older BY-NC-SA 2.0 license and recommending 4.0 upgrades to logically narrow the search to the corresponding 4.0 license (deep reasoning). It then requires identifying a second, more permissive attribution-only 4.0 license and aggregating detailed terms, URLs, permissions, restrictions, and disclaimers from both licenses (wide information synthesis across multiple primary sources).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Canonical URL https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/ See the legal code You are free to: Share — copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format Adapt —
rounds.answer_a:
The license preserving the original elements is **Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**, found at `https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/` [1]. It grants the freedoms to **Share** (copy and redistribute in any medium) and **Adapt** (remix, transform, and build upon the material) [1]. **Commercial use is not permitted**, and the **ShareAlike** condition requires derivative works to be licensed under identical terms. The "No Additional Restrictions" clause prohibits users from applying legal terms or technological measures that legally restrict others from exercising the license rights [1].

The license requiring only attribution is **Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0)**, located at `https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/` [2]. It allows users to Share and Adapt [2]. **Commercial use is allowed**, and the **ShareAlike** requirement is not applied. It similarly enforces the "No Additional Restrictions" clause to protect downstream user rights [2].

Both licenses contain a standard legal notice stating the deed is a human-readable summary of the Legal Code and is not a license itself. The disclaimer clarifies that Creative Commons is not a law firm and does not provide legal services. Additionally, it states the work is provided "as-is" without warranties, and that the licensor does not grant rights regarding the use of the work that would constitute copyright infringement or violate other rights [1, 2].

## References
[1] Creative Commons, "Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International," https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
[2] Creative Commons, "Attribution 4.0 International," https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/
rounds.answer_b:
The **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 International**[1] license preserves the core elements of the CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 license. Its canonical URL is https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/. This license grants freedoms to share and adapt the material for noncommercial purposes only[1]. Commercial use is prohibited, share-alike is required for adaptations, and the "no additional restrictions" clause prohibits imposing legal or technological measures that restrict others' exercise of licensed rights[1].

The **CC BY 4.0 International**[2] license requires only attribution and permits commercial use without share-alike requirements. Its canonical URL is https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/. This license grants freedoms to share and adapt material for any purpose, including commercial[2]. Commercial use is permitted, share-alike is not required, and the "no additional restrictions" clause applies identically[2].

Both licenses include the standard legal notice: "This is a human-readable summary of (and not a substitute for) the license," clarifying that Creative Commons is not a law firm and provides no legal counsel[1][2]. The deeds specify that Creative Commons is not party to the licenses and assumes no responsibility for content and related information licensed under them[1][2]. Neither license provides warranties; licensors offer material "as-is" without representations or warranties of any kind[1][2].

The share-alike requirement distinguishes CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, obligating derivative works to use identical or compatible licenses, whereas CC BY 4.0 imposes no such condition. The noncommercial restriction in CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 contrasts sharply with CC BY 4.0's allowance of commercial exploitation. Both preserve core attribution requirements and adopt the robust freedoms framework of the 4.0 generation.

## References

[1] https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
[2] https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the two required licenses (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 and CC BY 4.0) and provided accurate canonical URLs. Both satisfied the Deep Logic requirement and covered the Width checklist: freedoms (share/adapt), commercial use conditions, ShareAlike requirement (or absence), the "No additional restrictions" clause, and the standard deed/disclaimer language (deed not a license, CC not a law firm, no warranties). 

However, Agent B provides slightly stronger user experience: clearer structural separation of the two licenses, more explicit phrasing of the freedoms (including commercial use under CC BY 4.0), and a more complete disclaimer summary (including that Creative Commons is not a party to the license and assumes no responsibility). Agent B also adds a concise comparative paragraph clarifying the distinction between the two licenses, improving scannability and comprehension. 

Agent A is accurate and solid but slightly less structured and marginally less comprehensive in its disclaimer explanation. Since there are no factual failures from either agent, the decision is based on presentation and completeness. Therefore, B is better on user utility and formatting, with no checklist failures from A.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question:
You are preparing a UNIX/Linux tutorial for beginners and want to release it under a ShareAlike Creative Commons license so others can adapt and redistribute it. To ensure compliance when others remix your work, analyze the official compatibility rules for the current 4.0 versions of the two ShareAlike licenses (the commercial and the noncommercial variants). 

First, identify which non-CC licenses (if any) have been officially declared compatible with the 4.0 version of the commercial ShareAlike license, and explain any special conditions that apply to those compatibilities. Then clarify whether any non-CC licenses are currently compatible with the 4.0 version of the noncommercial ShareAlike license. 

Next, describe the mission of the nonprofit organization that stewards these licenses and summarize the types of tools it provides to support the global knowledge commons. 

Finally, if you wanted formal training before licensing and distributing your Linux tutorial, outline the main certificate tracks offered by this organization, including their target audiences, course structure (duration and format), and the available bootcamp delivery options.
checklist_width: Identification of Free Art License 1.3 as compatible with BY-SA 4.0
Identification of GNU GPLv3 as compatible with BY-SA 4.0
Explanation that GPLv3 compatibility is one-way only
Statement that no non-CC licenses are currently compatible with BY-NC-SA 4.0
Description of the nonprofit’s mission to nurture and defend a global commons of shared knowledge and culture
Explanation that the organization provides standardized copyright licenses and public domain tools
Listing of the three main certificate tracks (Educators, Academic Librarians, Open Culture)
Description of course structure (10-week online courses, cohort-based, microcredentials)
Description of bootcamp options (intensive workshops, in-person one-week or online one- or two-week formats)
checklist_depth: Free Art License 1.3 + officially designated as a BY-SA 4.0 compatible license
GNU General Public License v3 + designated compatible with BY-SA 4.0 with one-way compatibility
No non-CC licenses compatible with BY-NC-SA 4.0 + confirmed by official compatibility list
Creative Commons + nonprofit steward of the licenses and commons mission
CC Certificate program + includes Educators, Academic Librarians, and Open Culture tracks
rounds.rationale: The question is anchored in the scenario of publishing a UNIX/Linux beginner tutorial under ShareAlike licensing (domain relevance), but it masks the specific source by referring generally to official compatibility rules and training programs. The 'Deep' component requires identifying the precise compatibility logic of BY-SA 4.0 versus BY-NC-SA 4.0 and distinguishing one-way GPL compatibility. The 'Wide' component forces aggregation across multiple domains: license compatibility lists, organizational mission and tools, and detailed certificate program structures and bootcamp formats. Answering fully requires synthesizing information scattered across multiple official Creative Commons resources.
source_nodes: The commons belongs to us all - Creative Commons
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: This is the list of licenses that have been approved by Creative Commons as compatible with the two Creative Commons ShareAlike licenses, CC BY-SA and CC BY-NC-SA. You must us
rounds.answer_a:
For the commercial **Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-SA 4.0)**, the official non-CC compatible licenses include the **Free Art License (FAL 1.3)** and the **GNU General Public License (GPL v3)** [1]. This compatibility permits one-way porting of material, but users must strictly adhere to special conditions. Specifically, when incorporating GPL v3 works, users are required to remove any "Invariant Sections," cover texts, or legal terms that would legally restrict others from exercising the rights granted by the CC BY-SA license [1]. Conversely, the **Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)** currently has **no officially declared compatible non-CC licenses**, meaning derivative works cannot be ported to other external licenses [1].

Creative Commons (CC) is a nonprofit network dedicated to overcoming legal obstacles to sharing ideas and creativity. Its mission is to cultivate a thriving, collaborative global commons by providing free, easy-to-use legal tools and technical infrastructure that maximize accessibility [2]. To support this, CC offers copyright licenses, public domain tools like CC0, and search platforms such as Openverse to facilitate the discovery and reuse of open content globally [2].

For formal training, the **CC Certificate** program offers specialized tracks for **Educators, Librarians, and GLAM (Galleries, Libraries, Archives, and Museums) professionals** [3]. The standard course is structured as a **10-week online cohort** that blends synchronous Zoom classes with asynchronous readings and peer discussions on copyright and open licensing [3]. Furthermore, **Bootcamp delivery options** are available to accelerate learning. These bootcamps condense the full curriculum into intensive, multi-day sessions (often one week), allowing individuals and teams to rapidly gain certification and expertise in open practices [3].

## References
[1] Creative Commons, "4.0 License Compatibility," https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/licensing-considerations/compatible-licenses
[2] Creative Commons, "About Creative Commons," https://creativecommons.org/about/
[3] Creative Commons, "Certificate Program," https://certificates.creativecommons.org/
rounds.answer_b:
The **CC BY-SA 4.0** license has no officially declared compatible non-CC licenses for two-way compatibility, though one-way compatibility exists for older CC licenses[1]. The **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0** license similarly has no currently compatible non-CC licenses declared in Creative Commons' official compatibility database[1].

Creative Commons is a nonprofit organization stewarding global licensing infrastructure to maximize digital knowledge sharing[2]. Its mission centers on helping creators share work and build upon others' contributions legally and securely. The organization provides free legal tools including six primary license suites (ranging from full freedom to attribution-only requirements), the public domain CC0 designation, and the Community License Framework enabling custom tailoring[2][3]. Additional resources include the CC Search tool for finding openly licensed content, rights expression infrastructure, and policy advocacy supporting open access globally[2].

Creative Commons offers multiple certificate tracks designed for diverse audiences[4]. The **Copyright for Creatives** track targets content creators, artists, and non-legal professionals seeking foundational knowledge in approximately 4-6 weeks via self-paced online modules[4]. The **Copyright for Educators** track serves teachers and educational administrators, teaching practical classroom applications over similar timeframes[4]. The **Copyright for Developers** track, structured for software engineers and technologists, covers licensing implications for code and data projects[4].

Each certificate includes interactive coursework, practical case studies, and culminating assessments[4]. Creative Commons offers bootcamp delivery models—typically 2-3 day intensive formats—for organizations seeking compressed training timelines, with customizable content addressing institutional licensing requirements[4]. Bootcamps combine instructor-led sessions with hands-on licensing scenario work, enabling groups to rapidly upskill before implementing organizational open licensing strategies[4].

These certificates provide formal preparation before releasing tutorials under specific Creative Commons licenses, ensuring creators understand attribution mechanics, derivative work requirements under share-alike provisions, and compliance obligations their chosen license imposes on downstream users and remixers[4].

## References

[1]https://creativecommons.org/about/cclicenses/
[2]https://creativecommons.org/about/
[3]https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/
[4]https://creativecommons.org/get-certified/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies Free Art License 1.3 and GNU GPLv3 as compatible with CC BY-SA 4.0 and correctly states that no non-CC licenses are compatible with BY-NC-SA 4.0. Agent B incorrectly claims there are no compatible non-CC licenses for BY-SA 4.0, failing the core compatibility question. 

Width/Completeness: Agent A covers most required elements (FAL 1.3, GPLv3 one-way compatibility, no BY-NC-SA compatibility, CC’s mission, licenses and public domain tools, 10-week cohort structure, bootcamp intensives). However, it slightly misstates GPL conditions and does not precisely list the three official certificate tracks (Educators, Academic Librarians, Open Culture). Agent B misses major checklist items: it omits FAL 1.3 and GPLv3 compatibility entirely and lists incorrect certificate tracks, failing multiple required elements.

Presentation & UX: Agent A is clearly structured, uses formatting, and provides a coherent, scan-friendly explanation aligned with the query. Agent B is readable but built on incorrect core facts, which severely undermines user trust and usefulness.

Because Agent A has minor factual imprecision (GPL conditions, certificate naming), it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. However, Agent B fails both deep logic and wide checklist coverage. Therefore, Agent A is BETTER.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: BOTH.

rounds.question: You are preparing to publish a free “UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners” and want to ensure it can be legally shared, remixed, and reused worldwide. Identify the nonprofit organization that both (a) develops and stewards the globally used open licenses and public domain tools commonly applied to educational and technical materials, and (b) operates a structured certificate training program with sector-specific courses (including for educators, academic librarians, and cultural heritage professionals). Based on publicly available information, explain this organization’s mission, describe its core legal and technical tools (including its standard licenses, public domain dedication/marking tools, and its newer framework for guiding AI reuse), and outline how it supports implementation across sectors through training, institutional adoption, and capacity-building resources. Answer length: 300-400 words.
checklist_width: Clear statement of the organization’s mission to enable global sharing of knowledge and creativity
Description of the standard open licenses and their global use
Explanation of CC0 and the Public Domain Mark (public domain dedication/identification tools)
Description of the AI-related framework (CC Signals) and its purpose
Overview of the certificate training program and its sector-specific courses (Educators, Academic Librarians, Open Culture)
Details about implementation support: institutional adoption, training, guidance, best practices, and search/access tools
checklist_depth: Creative Commons identified as the nonprofit that builds and stewards CC Licenses, CC0, Public Domain Mark, and CC Signals
Creative Commons identified as the organization that offers the CC Certificate program with sector-specific courses and global training initiatives
rounds.rationale: The question uses a Linux tutorial publishing scenario to anchor relevance to the UNIX/Linux domain while masking the entity’s name. The deep logic requires recognizing the single organization that both maintains globally adopted open licensing tools (licenses, CC0, public domain mark, AI signals) and runs a formal certificate training program for educators, librarians, and cultural professionals. The wide scope requires aggregating mission, legal tools, AI framework, training programs, and implementation support details from multiple public sources about the same organization.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Creative Commons builds and maintains open tools that help creators and institutions share knowledge and creativity legally and globally. Our tools, like the CC licenses and p
rounds.answer_a:
Creative Commons (CC) is the global nonprofit organization dedicated to developing and stewarding the open licenses and public domain tools essential for legally sharing educational and technical materials. Its mission is to overcome legal obstacles to the sharing of knowledge and creativity, cultivating a vibrant, collaborative global commons where universal access to research and education is the norm [1]. CC’s legal infrastructure features six standardized copyright licenses, ranging from the open Attribution (CC BY) to the restrictive Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivs (CC BY-NC-ND), as well as public domain tools like CC0 and the Public Domain Mark [2]. To address modern challenges, CC has established a comprehensive AI strategy framework. This framework guides the application of CC licenses regarding AI training data and machine-generated outputs, while advocating for open data ecosystems, transparent infrastructure, and robust regulations to ensure AI development remains accountable and accessible to the public [3].

CC supports the practical application of these tools through its CC Certificate program, a formal training offering designed for Educators, Librarians, and GLAM (Galleries, Libraries, Archives, and Museums) professionals [4]. This program builds sector-specific capacity through standardized, expert-led courses that cover deep legal nuances of open licensing, copyright, and community engagement strategies. Beyond individual certification, CC fosters institutional adoption through global initiatives like "Open Education" and "Open GLAM," providing the policy frameworks and resources necessary for organizations to implement open practices [5]. Additionally, CC offers technical resources such as Openverse, a search engine for open content, and operates the CC Global Network. This network empowers local communities and chapters worldwide, driving the advocacy and on-the-ground support required to sustain the knowledge commons across diverse regions and sectors [5].

## References
[1] Creative Commons, "About," https://creativecommons.org/about/
[2] Creative Commons, "Legal Tools," https://creativecommons.org/legal-tools/
[3] Creative Commons, "CC AI Strategy," https://creativecommons.org/ai/
[4] Creative Commons, "Certificate Program," https://certificates.creativecommons.org/
[5] Creative Commons, "Global Network," https://network.creativecommons.org/
rounds.answer_b:
**Creative Commons** is the nonprofit organization that develops and stewards open licenses and public domain tools globally used for educational and technical materials[1]. Founded in 2001, Creative Commons advances its mission to maximize digital knowledge sharing by providing free, standardized legal frameworks enabling creators to share work while maintaining appropriate attribution and usage controls[1][2].

Creative Commons' core legal tools include six primary license suites ranging from CC0 (full public domain dedication) to CC BY-SA (requiring attribution and share-alike compliance), plus the CC BY license permitting commercial reuse with attribution only[1][2]. For public domain marking, Creative Commons provides the CC0 Public Domain Dedication and the Public Domain Mark, enabling creators to waive rights or identify works already in the public domain[1][3]. These tools have achieved near-universal adoption across open educational resources, academic repositories, cultural heritage institutions, and government data initiatives[2].

Recognizing emerging AI challenges, Creative Commons developed new guidance frameworks addressing AI training and reuse considerations[1]. In 2024, Creative Commons introduced updated language and resources helping creators understand how their works may be used in AI systems and enabling informed licensing choices reflecting their values around machine learning applications[1].

The organization supports global implementation through tiered capacity-building initiatives. Its certification program offers sector-specific tracks including **Copyright for Educators** (serving K-12 and higher education faculty), **Copyright for Academic Librarians** (supporting institutional repository management and open scholarship), and **Copyright for Cultural Heritage Professionals** (enabling museums, archives, and GLAM institutions to license collections)[2][4]. Certificates combine self-paced online modules with practical assessments, typically requiring 4-8 weeks of engagement[4].

Beyond individual training, Creative Commons operates institutional adoption programs working directly with universities, libraries, government agencies, and international organizations to implement organizational open licensing strategies[2]. The organization provides technical infrastructure through CC Search, enabling discovery of openly licensed content across millions of works, and contributes to global policy advocacy supporting open access in education, research, and cultural heritage sectors[1][2].

Creative Commons' integrated approach—combining accessible legal tools, practical training, technical platforms, and policy engagement—establishes the infrastructure enabling creators like yourself to share tutorials globally while preserving attribution rights and supporting downstream remix and reuse[1][2].

## References

[1] https://creativecommons.org/about/
[2] https://creativecommons.org/
[3] https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/
[4] https://creativecommons.org/get-certified/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identify Creative Commons as the nonprofit that stewards the CC licenses, CC0, Public Domain Mark, and operates the CC Certificate program. Both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A more accurately describes the six standard CC licenses and correctly distinguishes them from CC0 and the Public Domain Mark as separate public domain tools. Agent B incorrectly characterizes "six primary license suites ranging from CC0," conflating CC0 (a public domain dedication tool) with the license suite—this is a factual imprecision. Both mention AI-related guidance, but neither explicitly names "CC Signals," which was part of the checklist; however, Agent A’s description of an AI strategy framework is slightly clearer and more aligned with Creative Commons’ current public positioning. Both adequately cover the certificate program (with sector-specific tracks) and implementation support (institutional adoption, training, search tools, global network), though Agent A provides stronger detail on Openverse and the Global Network.

Third, User Experience & Presentation: Agent B uses more visible formatting (bold text), which improves scannability. However, Agent A provides slightly more precise legal distinctions and a more comprehensive description of implementation infrastructure. Since accuracy is foundational and Agent A avoids the license mischaracterization seen in Agent B, A delivers the stronger overall user experience despite plainer formatting.

Conclusion: Agent A wins on factual precision and slightly stronger completeness. The loss for Agent B is a WIDE aggregation issue (license mischaracterization and slightly less precise AI framework coverage), not a deep logic failure.
